label: Reporting Person May Escalate from Internal to External Channel
description: If a reporting person first uses the internal reporting channel and does not receive adequate follow-up within 3 months (or 7 days acknowledgement + 3 months feedback), they may report to the competent authority's external channel and are entitled to the same protections as direct external reporters.
trigger: No adequate response received from obligated entity within 3 months of internal report, or reporting person reasonably believes internal report will not lead to effective follow-up or risks retaliation
outcome: The reporting person is fully protected for the external report; the competent authority must follow up and provide feedback within 3 months (extendable to 6).

label: Reporting Person May Escalate to Public Disclosure After Inadequate External Follow-Up
description: If a reporting person has reported through external channels and no appropriate action was taken within 3 months, or if there is no competent authority with an external channel, or if there is an imminent or manifest public interest danger, the reporting person may make a public disclosure and retain full protection.
trigger: One of three conditions met: (1) no adequate action taken on external report within 3 months; (2) no competent authority exists for the subject matter; (3) imminent/manifest public danger justifies bypassing internal/external channels.
outcome: Public disclosure is protected. The reporting person is immune from civil, criminal, administrative, and disciplinary liability if acting in good faith with reasonable grounds.
human_review_note: The three conditions for protected public disclosure each require case-by-case factual analysis. The concept of 'imminent or manifest public danger' is not precisely defined in the Directive and will require national jurisprudence to clarify. Human review recommended.

label: Competent Authority Forwards Out-of-Competence Reports to Appropriate Authority
description: When a competent authority receives a report that falls outside its remit, it must promptly forward it to the appropriate competent national authority or to a relevant EU body (OLAF, EPPO, etc.), and inform the reporting person of the referral.
trigger: Report received by a competent authority falls outside its material or territorial competence.
outcome: The forwarded report is handled by the receiving authority under the same rules. The reporting person retains protection throughout.

label: Obligated Entity May Refer Serious Breaches to Competent Authorities
description: When follow-up on an internal report reveals a serious breach of Union law, the obligated entity should (or in some Member States must) refer the matter to the relevant competent authority, while maintaining the reporting person's confidentiality.
trigger: Internal investigation reveals a breach of Union law that warrants regulatory or law enforcement action.
outcome: Competent authority takes over investigation; obligated entity must not retaliate against the reporting person for the report that triggered the referral.
human_review_note: The Directive does not explicitly require obligated entities to refer breaches to authorities, but encourages it through the overall framework. Whether national law imposes a mandatory referral obligation depends on national transposition. Human review recommended.

label: Facilitators and Third Parties Are Protected from Retaliation
description: Persons who assist the reporting person (facilitators) and third parties connected to the reporting person (relatives, colleagues, legal entities they own) are also protected from retaliation taken as a consequence of the report.
trigger: A facilitator or third party suffers adverse treatment in a work-related context as a consequence of the reporting person's report.
outcome: The same anti-retaliation protections, remedies, and reversed burden of proof apply as for the reporting person themselves.

label: Rights of the Concerned Person Must Be Preserved Throughout Proceedings
description: All procedures for handling reports must fully protect the fundamental rights of the concerned person, including presumption of innocence, right to a fair trial, and right to an effective remedy. The identity of the concerned person must also be protected from disclosure until a formal investigation is concluded, unless required by law.
trigger: A report is submitted naming or identifying a concerned person.
outcome: Obligated entities and competent authorities must handle report data about the concerned person with strict confidentiality and procedural fairness.
